FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes cross-sectional data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study at Year 2 (2018–2020), explicitly stated in Methods: 'We conducted a secondary cross-sectional data analysis from the 2-year follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study'

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Methods describe original data collection using Modified Poisson regression analyses to examine associations between cyberbullying and eating disorder symptoms in 10,258 ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2023), default presumption met.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying victimization and perpetration (yes/no questions about being bullied 'online, in texts, or group texts, or on social media') but does NOT measure phone/SM time or usage as a separate variable. No Step 1 phone/SM-specific estimate (MPIQ, SMAQ, time-on-platform, etc.) found in results. Step 2 not applicable—no composite constructed. Per binding clarification §2.4: 'Cyberbullying alone does not qualify' unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable with its own coefficient.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Cyberbullying-only exposure with no separately-measured phone/SM variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: This is the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort from the Netherlands (Van Eijsden et al., 2010), not the U.S. ABCD Study. Methods state: 'The community sample consisted of 2663 Dutch adolescents (54% girls)...from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study.' The criteria explicitly exclude this cohort: 'Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (Dutch ABCD)...NOT_MET.'

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC1. Non-U.S. ABCD cohort (Dutch ABCD).

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes data from the U.S. ABCD Study. Methods: 'Secondary data analyses were conducted using data from baseline through 2-year follow-up of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study (4.0 release).' Baseline 2016–2018, ages 9–10 years, 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis. Methods describe original regression analyses examining associations between ACEs, bullying victimization, and internalizing/externalizing problems in 8,085 ABCD participants.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Youth and Adolescence (2024), default presumption met.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures bullying victimization (cyberbullying yes/no question and traditional bullying via Peer Experiences Questionnaire) and pairs with mental health outcomes (internalizing/externalizing problems via Child Behavior Checklist). No phone/SM-specific variable measured. Step 1 search yields no separate MPIQ, SMAQ, time-on-SM, time-on-phone, or platform-specific estimate. Step 2 not applicable—no composite constructed. Per binding clarification §2.4: 'Cyberbullying alone does not qualify' unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable. Bullying victimization without separate phone/SM measurement fails IC2.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Bullying victimization-only with no separately-measured phone/SM variable.
